sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Diphtheria is a serious, potentially fatal infection caused by toxin-producing *Corynebacterium diphtheriae*. The toxin causes local tissue necrosis (pseudomembrane) and systemic complications including myocarditis and neuropathy. Case fatality rate for respiratory diphtheria is 5–10% even with treatment.

**Incubation:** 2–5 days (range up to 10 days)

**Infectious period:** Variable; untreated cases average 18.5 days. Effective antibiotics reduce shedding to ~5 days. Consider infectious from 7 days prior to symptom onset until clearance cultures obtained.

**Transmission:** Respiratory droplets or direct contact with respiratory secretions/wound exudate. Prolonged close contact usually required.

**MANDATORY NOTIFIABLE DISEASE in Western Australia** — report on suspicion. One case in a remote mine site or community is a public health emergency.

**Pilbara / Kimberley Context:** Current outbreak activity. High index of suspicion in unvaccinated or under-vaccinated persons, Indigenous communities, and contacts of known cases.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
### Hallmark Sign
Grey-white **pseudomembrane** on tonsils/pharynx — leathery, tough, adherent. **Bleeds when you try to wipe it off.** May extend to soft palate, uvula, or larynx.

### Early Symptoms (Days 1–2)
- Mild sore throat — often less painful than it looks
- Low-grade fever 38–38.5°C — **patient looks sicker than the temperature suggests**
- Fatigue, malaise, loss of appetite
- Muffled or "hot potato" voice

### Progressing Disease (Days 2–4)
- **Bull neck** — dramatic cervical swelling from lymphadenopathy and tissue oedema
- Hoarse voice, barking cough (laryngeal involvement)
- Nasal discharge, possibly blood-tinged (nasal diphtheria)
- Membrane spreads, becomes more visible
- Dysphagia, drooling

### Severe / Late Complications
| Complication | Timing | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Airway obstruction | Days 2–5 | From membrane extension and oedema |
| Myocarditis | ~1 week | Arrhythmias, conduction defects, cardiomyopathy |
| Neuropathy | Weeks–months | Cranial nerve palsy, respiratory muscle weakness, peripheral |
| Renal failure | Variable | Toxin-mediated |
| Death | 5–10% CFR | Higher if untreated or treatment delayed |

### Danger Signs — Act Immediately
- Stridor or respiratory distress
- Cyanosis
- Drooling / inability to swallow
- Arrhythmia or chest pain
- Airway obstruction

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
### Epidemiological Red Flags
- Unvaccinated or unknown vaccination status
- Contact with Kimberley/Pilbara region or travellers from outbreak area
- Multiple cases in same household or community
- Remote Indigenous community (potential vaccination gaps)
- Overseas-born workers from endemic regions

### Differential Diagnosis
| Condition | Key Differentiators |
|---|---|
| Strep tonsillitis | Membrane wipes off cleanly without bleeding; higher fever |
| Glandular fever (EBV) | Membrane softer, white; different lymph node pattern; splenomegaly |
| Viral croup | Barking cough but NO membrane, NO bull neck |
| Peritonsillar abscess | Unilateral swelling, fluctuant, no membrane, uvula deviated |
| Epiglottitis | Drooling, tripod posture, high fever, rapid onset; no membrane on throat exam |

### PPE Requirements
- **Droplet precautions** (surgical mask, eye protection, gloves, gown)
- Apply surgical mask to patient if tolerated
- Isolate patient immediately on suspicion

### Mine Site / Remote Context
- Limited airway management options — do NOT attempt to remove membrane
- DAT unlikely available on site — must be requested via RFDS evacuation or WA Health
- Antibiotic resistance to penicillin emerging — azithromycin preferred first-line
- Coordinate with WA Country Health and RFDS early
- Contact tracing critical — identify all close contacts in shared accommodation, dining, vehicles

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
### Immediate Actions (On Suspicion)

1. **ISOLATE** — Droplet precautions. Single room. Mask the patient.
2. **NOTIFY** — Call WA Health immediately. Diphtheria is mandatory notifiable on clinical suspicion.
3. **CALL RFDS** — Request evacuation. Request diphtheria antitoxin (DAT) on the aircraft.
4. **AIRWAY** — Maintain vigilance. **Do NOT attempt to remove or dislodge the membrane** (risk of haemorrhage and complete airway obstruction). Position of comfort. Have suction, oxygen, and airway adjuncts ready.
5. **ANTIBIOTICS** — Start immediately (do not wait for lab confirmation).

### Antibiotic Therapy (WHO 2024 Recommendation)
**First-line: Macrolide antibiotics preferred over penicillin**

| Agent | Adult Dose | Paediatric Dose | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Azithromycin** (preferred) | 500 mg PO/IV daily | 10 mg/kg (max 500 mg) PO/IV daily | 7 days |
| Erythromycin (alternative) | 500 mg PO/IV 6-hourly | 10 mg/kg (max 500 mg) 6-hourly | 14 days |

*Note: Penicillin resistance emerging in Australian outbreak strains (NT, QLD). Azithromycin is preferred first-line.*

### Supportive Care
- Oxygen if required — titrate to SpO₂ ≥94%
- IV access — anticipate fluid requirements and medication administration
- Cardiac monitoring — continuous if available (risk of arrhythmia)
- Nil by mouth if airway concerns
- Analgesia / antipyretics as required
- Strict fluid balance

### Key Contacts
| Service | Number |
|---|---|
| RFDS Perth 24/7 | 1800 625 800 |
| WA Country Health On-Call | 1800 633 592 |
| Poisons / Emergency Advice | 13 11 26 |

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
### Diphtheria Antitoxin (DAT)
DAT is the mainstay of treatment for respiratory diphtheria. It neutralises circulating toxin but **cannot reverse toxin already bound to tissue** — early administration is critical.

**WHO 2024: Do NOT perform routine sensitivity testing before administration** (delays outweigh risk of rare anaphylaxis). Administer promptly.

**Escalating Dose Regimen (WHO 2024 / UKHSA):**

| Severity / Presentation | DAT Dose (IU) |
|---|---|
| Laryngeal/pharyngeal disease <48 hours | 20,000–40,000 |
| Nasopharyngeal disease | 40,000–60,000 |
| Severe: bull neck, extensive membrane, disease >48 hours | 80,000–100,000 |

- **Same dose for adults and children**
- Single dose — do not repeat
- Administer IV: mix in 250–500 mL normal saline, infuse slowly over 2–4 hours
- Give 10% of volume over first 30 minutes, remainder over 2–4 hours
- Monitor closely for anaphylaxis — have adrenaline, resuscitation equipment ready
- DAT is equine-derived — anaphylaxis rare but possible

**Obtaining DAT in WA:** Request via WA Health HCID On-Call or arrange on RFDS evacuation aircraft. Limited national supply.

### Airway Management
- Anticipate airway deterioration — membrane can extend and cause complete obstruction
- Prepare difficult airway equipment
- If intubation required, use smallest ETT that will pass — membrane narrows the lumen
- Surgical airway may be needed if oropharyngeal/laryngeal membrane prevents intubation
- Avoid blind suctioning of pharynx (may dislodge membrane)

### Cardiac Monitoring
- Continuous ECG monitoring for arrhythmias, conduction defects
- 12-lead ECG — look for PR prolongation, ST changes, T-wave inversion
- Myocarditis onset typically ~7 days — monitor even during recovery phase
- Troponin if available

### Complications Requiring Hospital Management
- Myocarditis / heart failure — may require inotropes, temporary pacing
- Neuropathy — respiratory muscle weakness may require ventilatory support
- Renal failure — monitor urine output, electrolytes
- Secondary bacterial infection

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
### Contact Management
- **High-risk contacts** (household, overnight, intimate): nasopharyngeal + throat swabs, prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin 500 mg daily × 5 days OR single IM benzathine penicillin if susceptible), vaccination if not up to date
- **Medium-risk contacts** (≥8 hours shared space): risk assessment, consider swabs and antibiotics
- **Low-risk contacts**: information and vaccination advice
- Symptom surveillance for 7 days from last exposure
- Contacts requiring antibiotics should avoid vulnerable populations until 72 hours of antibiotics completed or negative cultures obtained

### Clearance Criteria
- Two consecutive negative nasopharyngeal AND throat cultures, taken ≥24 hours apart, and >24 hours after completion of antibiotics
- If still positive after initial course: additional 10-day course of antibiotics

### Vaccination
- Diphtheria does not reliably confer immunity — vaccinate during convalescence
- Booster if >12 months since last dose (for cases and contacts)
- Primary or catch-up course if unvaccinated/incomplete
- If DAT given, delay vaccination by 4 weeks

### WA Notification Requirements
- **Mandatory notifiable disease** — report to WA Health immediately on clinical suspicion
- Do not wait for laboratory confirmation before notifying
- Single case triggers contact tracing and public health response

### References
1. World Health Organization. *Clinical management of diphtheria: guideline.* WHO; 2 February 2024. WHO-DIPH-Clinical-2024.1
2. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. *Clinical Guidance for Diphtheria.* CDC; updated May 12, 2025.
3. UK Health Security Agency. *Diphtheria anti-toxin: clinical guidance.* GOV.UK; issued May 2022, updated September 2025.
4. NSW Health. *Diphtheria control guideline.* Revised January 2024.
5. NT Health. *Clinical Management of Diphtheria for Primary Care in the Northern Territory: Interim Guidelines.* 2024.
6. Truelove SA, et al. *Clinical and epidemiological aspects of diphtheria: a systematic review and pooled analysis.* Clin Infect Dis. 2020;71:89–97.
7. NICD South Africa. *Diphtheria: NICD recommendations for diagnosis, management and public health response.* July 2025.
8. WHO Operational Protocol for Clinical Management of Diphtheria. 2017.
9. Australian Immunisation Handbook (current edition). Australian Government Department of Health.
